1: "In the LORD I take refuge. How then can you say to me: 'Flee like a bird to your mountain'?" — Christ as the refuge that makes flight unnecessary. Hebrews 6:18: those who have "fled to take hold of the hope set before us" — the imagery of fleeing to a city of refuge applied to the safety found in Christ. When crisis comes, the temptation is flight; Christ's answer to his disciples before Gethsemane (John 14:27 — "Do not let your hearts be troubled and do not be afraid") is the same confidence as this verse: the refuge is secure, flight unnecessary.
2: "For look, the wicked bend their bows; they set their arrows against the strings to shoot from the shadows at the upright in heart" — the hidden attacks of the enemy against Christ and his people. The shadow-attack typifies the Passion: the plotting in secret (Matthew 26:3-4 — "the chief priests and elders... plotted to arrest Jesus secretly"), the betrayal by one of his own (John 13:21-30). The attack from shadows is the enemy's preferred mode — light, which Christ is (John 8:12), exposes and defeats it.
3: "When the foundations are being destroyed, what can the righteous do?" — the crisis of foundational collapse. Christ's answer: "Heaven and earth will pass away, but my words will never pass away" (Matthew 24:35). When every visible foundation crumbles — temple, nation, social order — the words of Christ remain. The foundation that cannot be destroyed is not an institution but a person (1 Corinthians 3:11: "no one can lay any foundation other than the one already laid, which is Jesus Christ").
4: "The LORD is in his holy temple; the LORD is on his heavenly throne. He observes everyone on earth; his eyes examine them" — Christ's heavenly session after the ascension. Hebrews 8:1-2: "We do have such a high priest, who sat down at the right hand of the throne of the Majesty in heaven, and who serves in the sanctuary, the true tabernacle set up by the Lord." The risen Christ in the heavenly sanctuary sees and examines — his priestly observation is not surveillance but intercession (Hebrews 7:25: "he always lives to intercede for them").
5: "The LORD examines the righteous, but the wicked, those who love violence, he hates with a passion" — Christ's righteous examination of his people. Rev 2-3 (the letters to the seven churches) shows the risen Christ "examining" each congregation — seeing their works, commending their faithfulness, correcting their failures. The examination is not punitive but purifying: "I counsel you to buy from me gold refined in the fire, so you can become rich" (Revelation 3:18). Christ examines because he loves and desires righteousness in his people.
6: "On the wicked he will rain fiery coals and burning sulfur; a scorching wind will be their lot" — the coming judgment of Christ. 2 Thessalonians 1:7-9: "when the Lord Jesus is revealed from heaven in blazing fire with his powerful angels. He will punish those who do not know God... they will be punished with everlasting destruction." Revelation 14:10: those who receive the mark of the beast "will drink the wine of God's fury... they will be tormented with burning sulfur." The fiery judgment of Psalms 11:6 is the eschatological reality to which Christ will return.
7: "For the LORD is righteous, he loves justice; the upright will see his face" — Christ as both the one who loves justice and the one through whom the upright see the divine face. Matthew 5:8: "Blessed are the pure in heart, for they will see God." Revelation 22:4: "They will see his face, and his name will be on their foreheads." The vision of God's face — unmediated divine presence — was the supreme covenant aspiration (Exodus 33:18-23, where Moses could not see the face and live). Christ is both the one whose face is seen and the one who makes the seeing possible by purifying the heart (1 John 3:2: "when Christ appears, we shall be like him, for we shall see him as he is").
